coredata.dc:title: Europium-decorated graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for label-free, rapid and sensitive detection of Cu2+ and l-cysteine
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Liping
Song, Xinhong
Chen, Yiying
Rong, Mingcong
Wang, Yiru
Zhao, Li
Zhao, Tingting
Chen, Xi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, europium-decorated graphene quantum dots (Eu-GQDs) were prepared by treating three-dimensional Eu-decorated graphene (3D Eu-graphene) via a strong acid treatment. Various characterizations revealed that Eu atoms were successfully complexed with the oxygen functional groups on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with the atomic ratio of 2.54%. Compared with Eu free GQDs, the introduction of Eu atoms enhanced the electron density and improved the surface chemical activities of Eu-GQDs. Therefore, the obtained Eu-GQDs were used as a novel “off-on” fluorescent probe for the label-free determination of Cu2+ and l-cysteine (L-Cys) with high sensitivity and selectivity. The fluorescence intensity of Eu-GQDs was quenched in the presence of Cu2+ owing to the coordination reaction between Cu2+ and carboxyl groups on the surface of the Eu-GQDs. The fluorescence intensity of Eu-GQDs recovered with the subsequent addition of L-Cys because of the strong affinity of Cu2+ to L-Cys via the Cu–S bond. The experimental results showed that the fluorescence variation of the proposed approach had a good linear relationship in the range of 0.1–10 μM for Cu2+ and 0.5–50 μM for L-Cys with corresponding detection limits of 0.056 μM for Cu2+ and 0.31 μM for L-Cys. The current approach also displayed a special response to Cu2+ and L-Cys over the other co-existing metal ions and amino acids, and the results obtained from buffer-diluted serum samples suggested its applicability in biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Three-dimensional Eu-complexed graphene
Strong acid treatment
Europium-complexed graphene quantum dots
Change of electron density and surface chemical activities
Detection of Cu2+ and L-Cys

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ and L-Cys
Determination of L-Cys in buffer-diluted serum
Mechanism of the “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and L-Cys
Selectivity and sensitivity for the proposed approach to Cu2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of Eu-GQDs (Top inset: HRTEM image of an individual Eu-GQD; lower inset: diameter distribution of the Eu-GQDs.); b) AFM images (Inset: the height profile along the line in a topographic image of Eu-GQDs deposited on a fresh silicon slice. Scale bar: 4 μm) c) XRD pattern and d) Raman spectra of GQDs and Eu-GQDs.

a) FT-IR spectra of Eu-GQDs and GQDs; b) Full-scan XPS spectra of the Eu-GQDs and GQDs; c) The C 1s XPS spectrum of the Eu-GQDs; d) The N 1s XPS spectrum of the Eu-GQDs; e) The Eu 3d XPS spectrum of the Eu-GQDs; f) The Eu 4d XPS spectrum of the Eu-GQDs.

a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs and Eu-GQDs; b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu-GQDs with different excitation wavelengths (Inset: Photographs of Eu-GQD solution taken under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).).

a) Effect of different pH values on the detection of Cu2+ with Eu-GQDs; b) Selectivity of Eu-GQDs towards Cu2+ over other metal ions (The final concentration of each metal ion was 5 μM); c) Emission spectra of Eu-GQDs in the presence of various concentrations of Cu2+ in 0.2 M H3BO3–Na2B4O7 buffer solution (pH 7.0); d) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity variation and Cu2+ concentration (Inset: the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity variation and Cu2+ concentration in the range 0.1–10 μM). F0 and F1 are the fluorescence intensity of Eu-GQDs without and with Cu2+ in 0.2 M H3BO3–Na2B4O7 buffer solution (pH 7.0), respectively.

a) PL emission spectra of Eu-GQDs containing Cu2+ (60 μM) with the increasing addition of L-Cys concentrations (0.5–200 μM), b)The fluorescence intensity variation and L-Cys concentration. The inset shows linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity variation and L-Cys concentration over the range of 0.5–50 μM (F2 is the fluorescence intensity of the Eu-GQDs containing Cu2+ with the addition of L-Cys.).
Fluorescence response of the Eu-GQDs containing Cu2+ in the presence of different analytes. (The final concentration of each analyte was 20 μM).
Schematic illustration for the determination of Cu2+ and L-Cys using Eu-GQDs.
Detection of L-Cys in buffer-diluted calf and human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Europium-decorated graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for label-free, rapid and sensitive detection of Cu2+ and l-cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, Fuzhou, 350002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Science, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Science

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry and the MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, europium-decorated graphene quantum dots (Eu-GQDs) were prepared by treating three-dimensional Eu-decorated graphene (3D Eu-graphene) via a strong acid treatment. Various characterizations revealed that Eu atoms were successfully complexed with the oxygen functional groups on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with the atomic ratio of 2.54%. Compared with Eu free GQDs, the introduction of Eu atoms enhanced the electron density and improved the surface chemical activities of Eu-GQDs. Therefore, the obtained Eu-GQDs were used as a novel “off-on” fluorescent probe for the label-free determination of Cu2+ and l-cysteine (L-Cys) with high sensitivity and selectivity. The fluorescence intensity of Eu-GQDs was quenched in the presence of Cu2+ owing to the coordination reaction between Cu2+ and carboxyl groups on the surface of the Eu-GQDs. The fluorescence intensity of Eu-GQDs recovered with the subsequent addition of L-Cys because of the strong affinity of Cu2+ to L-Cys via the Cu–S bond. The experimental results showed that the fluorescence variation of the proposed approach had a good linear relationship in the range of 0.1–10 μM for Cu2+ and 0.5–50 μM for L-Cys with corresponding detection limits of 0.056 μM for Cu2+ and 0.31 μM for L-Cys. The current approach also displayed a special response to Cu2+ and L-Cys over the other co-existing metal ions and amino acids, and the results obtained from buffer-diluted serum samples suggested its applicability in biological samples.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Change of electron density and surface chemical activities

ce:section.ce:para: Since graphene has sparked great enthusiasm within the scientific community because of its unique physicochemical properties [1], as a zero-band-gap semiconductor, there is little chance to observe its luminescence, which is limited its optoelectronic application. Fortunately, both theoretical and experimental studies have shown that a proper electronic band in graphene could be rationally tailored by controlling its size, shape, and the sp2-hybridized distribution of graphene nanostructures [2,3]. Consequently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), known as photoluminescent graphene sheets with sizes smaller than 100 nm, have emerged and ignited tremendous research interest due to their outstanding photoluminescence properties, long term resistance to photobleaching, biocompatibility and low toxicity [4–6]. Up to now, various methods have been developed for the preparation of GQDs, and a great amount of effort has been devoted to the exploitation and property-regulating of pristine GQDs containing carbon and oxygen atoms only. Recent studies show that doping GQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen [7–10], boron [11,12], sulfur [13,14], fluorine [15] and chlorine [16] can effectively tune their band gap and electronic density as well as enhance their quantum yield and chemical activity, and these heteroatoms-doped GQDs have been applied in the fields of fuel cell development, optoelectronics, bioimaging and biosensors. Meanwhile, previous work reported that the optical properties of graphene could be tuned via the interactions between graphene and various atoms or molecules [17], and lanthanide atoms such as Er, Yb and Eu have been considered as ideal candidates for composites with graphene [18]. In line with the recent progresses of non-metal heteroatoms-doped GQDs, our interest in the performance of GQDs decorating with lanthanide atoms have been aroused.

Among lanthanide atoms, europium atoms (Eu) can be firstly considered because of their lower diffusion barrier and relatively higher adsorption energy than other rare earth ions such as gadolinium, ytterbium and neodymium [17]. Though there are only a few studies referring to Eu-decorated graphene [18–20], these studies indicate that Eu can modulate their photoluminescence behaviors and thus expand the applications of graphene in optoelectronics and biosensing. However, to our best knowledge, no effort has been made to synthesize Eu-GQDs and study on their unique optical properties.

The study of biologically important cations and l-cysteine (L-Cys) also has drawn great attention because of their vital roles in human metabolism. On the one hand, copper ion (Cu2+), the third most abundant essential transition metal ion after zinc and iron in the human body, is correlated with physiological activities such as cell respiration, biosynthesis and metabolism for all organisms. However, excess Cu2+ is associated with neurodegenerative diseases, gastrointestinal disturbance and damage to the liver and kidneys [21]. On the other hand, the level of L-Cys is related to slow-growth, liver damage, osteoporosis, cardiovascular diseases, Alzheimer's disease, etc [22–26]. Accordingly, the sensitive and quantitative determination of Cu2+ and L-Cys is of diagnostic importance and therapeutic implications. Up to date, fluorescent methods based on novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterials have aroused concern for their environmental-friendly characteristics, high sensitivity, and rapid and easy operation. Herein, in this work, we prepared Eu-GQDs by cutting 3D Eu-graphene using concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3. The obtained Eu-GQDs were water-soluble and emitted yellow luminescence under irradiation of 365 nm. Based on the fluorescence characteristics of Eu-GQDs, an “off-on” strategy for the determination of Cu2+ and L-Cys was developed. Since the electron density and surface chemical activities have been improved with the introduction of Eu atoms into GQDs, the fluorescence of Eu-GQDs was significantly quenched in the presence of Cu2+ owing to the coordinated reaction between Cu2+ and carboxyl groups on the surface of Eu-GQDs. The fluorescence of the Eu-GQDs was turned on with the subsequent addition of L-Cys, which was attributed to the formation of the Cu–S bond dissociating Cu2+ away from the surface of the Eu-GQDs. The fluorescence variation of Eu-GQDs appeared to show good linear relationships with the concentration of Cu2+ and L-Cys, and this could be used in the quantitative determination of both Cu2+ and L-Cys.

Graphite powder was purchased from the Lvyin Co. (Xiamen, China); potassium permanganate, concentrated sulfuric acid, sodium nitrate, homocysteine (Hcy), glutathione (GSH) and L-Cys from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China); 30% H2O2 from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tec Co., Ltd; and Eu2O3 was obtained from the Beijing HWRK Chem Co., Ltd. Stock solutions of the cations used in the experiments were prepared from their chloride or nitrate salts. All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Cellulose membrane dialysis tubing (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); serum samples were obtained from the First Affiliated Hospital of Xiamen University; and ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) from a Millipore Autopure WR600A system (Millipore Ltd., USA) was used throughout the experiments.

UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were acquired on a UV2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi), respectively. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 380 spectrophotometer (Thermo Electron Corp., U.S.A.). High resolution transmission microscopy (HRTEM) observations were performed on a TECNAI F-30 electron microscope (Philips-FEI, Netherlands) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were recorded on an Agilent 5500 SPM in the AC mode with silicon probes (NT-MDT, cantilever force constant 40 N m−1). Raman spectra were collected on the silicon substrate with a Nanophoton Raman 11 spectrometer (Japan, Nanophoton Co.; excitation wavelength: 532.00 nm; laser current: 100%; excitation power: 18.782870 mW). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from a Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Japan, Kuraray Co., LTD. N) with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 20 mA, λ = 1.54051 Å). X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system, Physical Electronics, U.S.A.) was used to investigate the functional groups present on the surface of the Eu-GQDs with all the binding energies calibrated using C 1s as reference energy (EC1s = 284.6 eV). Zeta potential was detected using a Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments, U.K.). All characterizations were carried out under ambient conditions.

Typically, EuCl3 was prepared by dissolving Eu2O3 in 0.01 M HCl solution and dried to obtain a white powder. Then, 3.0 mg of the EuCl3 was added into 20 mL 2 mg mL−1 graphene oxide (GO) solution synthesized from graphite powder using Hummers' method [27], and the mixture was subsequently treated ultrasonically for 1 h before it was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Next, the obtained 3D Eu-graphene was dried at 60 °C for 12 h [19]. Finally, the products were ground into powder for future use. A control experiment was carried out using only 20 mL 2 mg mL−1 GO solution.

Typically, 3 mL concentrated H2SO4 and 1 mL concentrated HNO3 were added to 3.0 mg 3D Eu-graphene, and the mixture was reflexed at 75 °C for 7.5 h. After cooling to room temperature, 1 M NaOH solution was added drop by drop into the obtained Eu-GQDs solution to adjust the pH to 1 under vigorous stirring, and then Na2CO3 powder was added to neutralize the solution pH to 7.0. Finally, the solution was subjected to dialysis to completely remove salts ions in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) overnight to obtain Eu-GQDs. The stock Eu-GQDs solution was obtained by concentration to 10 mL. Meanwhile, GQDs was also synthesized via the same experimental procedure with 3D graphene as source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ and L-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical run for the determination of Cu2+, 100 μL Eu-GQDs and 200 μL NaOH–H3BO3 buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 6.0) had different amounts of Cu2+ added at room temperature. The solutions were quickly mixed and diluted to 1 mL. After reaction for 10 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at a wavelength of 535 nm under an excitation wavelength at 490 nm.

In the determination of biothiols, 100 μL Eu-GQDs, 200 μL NaOH–H3BO3 buffer solution and 60 μL 1 μM Cu2+ were mixed and reacted at room temperature for 10 min. Then, different concentrations of L-Cys were added to the mixture and diluted to 1 mL using ultrapure water. After 10 min reaction, the fluorescence spectra were measured as in the above description for the determination of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of L-Cys in buffer-diluted serum
ce:section.ce:para: Based on previous reports [23,24], 0.1 mL fresh calf serum and human serum were diluted to 50 mL with phosphate buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.4). Then, a 4 μL buffer-diluted serum sample was taken out for L-Cys detection. Simultaneously, the solution was spiked with known concentrations of L-Cys (5 and 10 μM) following the standard addition method, and the recovery of L-Cys and the standard deviation values were obtained.

The Eu-GQDs were synthesized from 3D Eu-graphene via strong acid treatment as described in the Section 2.4. The as-prepared Eu-GQDs solution was homogeneous without any noticeable aggregation or color change even after 5 months at room temperature (Fig. S1a). TEM image showed that the Eu-GQDs were fairly uniform with diameters of ca. 2.3–5.4 nm (lower inset in Fig. 1 a), which was in good agreement with that of other heteroatom-doped GQDs [9,28]. The HRTEM image (top inset in Fig. 1a) indicated that the high crystallinity of the Eu-GQDs with a lattice parameter of 0.24 nm corresponding to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene. The AFM images (Fig. 1b) demonstrated that most of the Eu-GQDs consisted of 1–10 graphene layers with a topographic height profile of 0.5–3.5 nm.

The XRD pattern was carried out to examine the structure of GQDs and Eu-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, the XRD peak for Eu-GQDs shifted to a higher degree (ca. 24.5°) compared with that of GQDs (ca. 23.5°). The reduced interlayer spacing in Eu-GQDs (ca. 0.356 nm) could be attributed to the decrease of the hydrophilicity of GQDs resulting from the coordination between Eu atoms and the oxygen groups on the surface of the GQDs.

Fig. 1d showed the Raman spectra of GQDs and Eu-GQDs. There were two remarkable peaks at around 1363 and 1594 cm−1 corresponding to the well-defined D band and G band. As is known, the ratio of ID/IG in Raman spectra is used to evaluate the disorder in graphene materials, since the D band is related to the structural defects and partially disordered structures of the sp2 domains, and the G band depends on the E2g vibration mode of the sp2 carbon domains. Here, the ID/IG of Eu-GQDs (1.02) was larger than that of GQDs (0.99), suggesting a broken hexagonal symmetry of the graphene, most presumably by the heterogeneous introduction of Eu atoms.

The obtained Eu-GQDs were water-dispersible owing to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups at the edges and on the surface. The groups could be identified and confirmed using FTIR measurement and XPS characterization. As shown in Fig. 2 a, different oxygen-containing groups were observed in Eu-GQDs and GQDs such as C–OH stretching vibration at 3418 cm−1, CO vibration at 1632 cm−1 and 1718 cm−1, symmetric carboxylate stretch at 1389 cm−1 and epoxy group vibration at 1120 cm−1. Compared to the FTIR of GQDs, the change of CO vibration and the absorption band at 882 cm−1, 617 cm−1 and 444 cm−1 for Eu–O indicated that Eu atoms was coordinated to the graphene lattice via oxygen functionalities. Furthermore, XPS characterization of Eu-GQDs was carried out to investigate the chemical interactions between Eu atoms and GQDs. As displayed in Fig. 2b, in comparison with GQDs, there was the distinct presence of Eu 3d and Eu 4d in the XPS spectra of Eu-GQDs besides the C1s peak at ca. 284.5 eV and O1s peak at ca. 532.5 eV. The atomic ratio of C: O: Eu was 65.11%: 32.35%: 2.54%. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of the Eu-GQDs showed the presence of CC (284.2 eV), C–O (284.9 eV), CO (286.1 eV), and O–CO (288.4 eV) (Fig. 2c) and the O1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into CO (532.2 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (531 eV) (Fig. 2d), indicating the existence of O-rich groups corresponding to FTIR spectra (Fig. 2a). In addition, the Zeta potential of Eu-GQDs in neutral solution was −15.6 mV, which further confirmed the presence of carboxyl groups (Fig. S2). On the other hand, the peaks at ca. 1126.4 eV and 1131.8 eV in Fig. 2e belonged to Eu(II) 3d5/2 and Eu(III) 3d5/2 indicating the reduction of partial Eu(III) to Eu(II) during the hydrothermal process, and 135.6 eV in Fig. 2f belonged to Eu(III) 4d5/2 which was consistent with previous reports [18–20]. Therefore, the above results provided evidence for the presence of oxygen functionalities and their complexation to Eu atoms.

To explore the optical properties of Eu-GQDs, both the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence behaviors of Eu-GQDs were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, there were a typical absorption peak at ca. 205 nm attributed to the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds and another n–π* absorption band with a weak shoulder at ca. 260 nm [4]. Compared with those of GQDs, the π–π* absorption peak and n–π* absorption band of Eu-GQDs was red-shifted for 7 nm and blue-shifted for 15 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, there was also obvious shift of emission wavelength since the corresponding emission wavelength was 520 nm for GQDs and 535 nm for Eu-GQDs. The phenomena indicated that the introduction of heteroatoms played an important role in spectral shifts, which was in consistence with heteroatoms-doped carbon quantum dots in Kang's work [29]. And the photographic inset in Fig. 3b showed that the Eu-GQDs solution was pale-yellow, transparent and clear under daylight and emitted yellow fluorescence under excitation at 365 nm UV light. Similar to the other luminescent carbon nanoparticles, Eu-GQDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence emission. The emission wavelength of Eu-GQDs presented red shift with the obvious intensity decrease when the excitation wavelength changed from 410 to 530 nm, and the intensity of Eu-GQDs reached the maximum at 535 nm upon excitation at 490 nm (Fig. 3b). These fluorescence characteristics might have been caused by the different distributions of the isolated sp2-hybridized clusters and the surface energy trap [5]. Taking quinine sulfate in a 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution (quantum yield = 0.58) as a reference, we found that the quantum yield of Eu-GQDs was ca. 2.4%, while that of GQDs was 1.0%. The possible reason for the enhancement of quantum yield was that the electron transitions in the 4f orbitals of the introduced Eu atoms shortened the distance and energy level overlap between Eu atoms and sp2 clusters, which led to effective energy transfer from Eu atoms to the sp2 clusters. As a result, the radiative recombination rate increased significantly and the quantum yield of Eu-GQDs consequently enhanced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and L-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Since the electronegativity of Eu (1.19) is smaller than those of C (2.55) and O (3.44), and the atomic radius of Eu (231 pm) is larger than those of C (67 pm) and O (56 pm), it is supposed that the valence electrons of Eu in 4f orbitals are easy to lose, resulting in the enhancement of surface electron density of O atoms and the convenience of the coordination interaction between the Cu2+ and the oxygen groups on the surface of the Eu-GQDs. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of the Eu-GQDs was significantly quenched with the addition of 10 μM Cu2+, while the fluorescence intensity reduction of GQDs was less obvious under the same experimental conditions, confirming the crucial role of the introduction of Eu atoms in GQDs for the coordination interaction between Cu2+ and the oxygen groups on the surface of the Eu-GQDs. Therefore, as illustrated in Scheme 1 , we speculated that the fluorescence quenching of the Eu-GQDs with the addition of Cu2+ was attributed to the charge transfer process due to the coordination between Cu2+ and the oxygen groups on the surface of the Eu-GQDs. The gradual fluorescence intensity recovery of the Eu-GQDs with the sequential addition of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) (Fig. S5) and the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-GQDs (2.70 ns) reduced to 2.02 ns with the addition of 10 μM Cu2+ and even to 1.12 ns in the presence of 60 μM Cu2+ (Fig. S6). These results proved the coordination interaction and electron transfer process between Cu2+ and Eu-GQDs.

Based on the experimental result, we considered that L-Cys with special thiol groups would show strong affinity to Cu2+ through a Cu–S bond. As anticipated, upon the addition of L-Cys, the fluorescence intensity recovered owing to the stronger interaction between Cu2+ and the L-Cys, which successfully removed Cu2+ away from the surface of the Eu-GQDs. Inspired by these phenomena, an “off-on” fluorescent approach using Eu-GQDs was proposed for the determination of Cu2+ and L-Cys.

In view of the effect of the acidity on the determination of Cu2+, the experiment under the condition of different pH solution was studied in this work. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the solution pH greatly affected the fluorescence quenching of Cu2+ in 0.2 M H3BO3–Na2B4O7 buffer solution. The optimal pH for the determination of Cu2+ was found to be pH 7, which could be ascribed to the protonation of the carbonyl groups and hydroxyl groups on the surface of Eu-GQDs in acidic condition, and the hydrolyzation of Cu2+ to Cu(OH)2 at a higher pH value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity for the proposed approach to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal pH condition, a series of metal ions including Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Al3+ and Cr6+ (the concentration of each ion was 5 μM) were chosen to evaluate the specificity of the proposed approach to Cu2+ in the experimental procedure. As shown in Fig. 4b, Eu-GQDs showed high selectivity towards Cu2+ over the other cations. Next, the sensitivity of the quantitative determination of Cu2+ was investigated (Fig. 4c, d). A good linear correlation could be achieved between the fluorescence intensity and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0.1–10 μM. The linear regression equation was F0–F1 = 4.33 + 88.1 CCu 2+ (μM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9941 and a detection limit of 0.056 μM Cu2+, which was very much lower than the level applied (1.3 ppm, ca. 20 mM) by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency for drinking water [21], adding promise to the possibility of using the proposed approach in practical applications. Moreover, the linear range and detection limit was comparable with or superior to those obtained from other sensitive methods (Table S1).

Previous reports show that L-Cys can react with Cu2+ through the Cu–S bond [22,26], therefore, the Eu-GQDs-Cu2+ system was explored for L-Cys sensing based on the competition mechanism between thiol groups of L-Cys and the oxygen groups on the surface of Eu-GQDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of Eu-GQDs recovered gradually with the addition of L-Cys. The fluorescence intensity variation of the Eu-GQDs-Cu2+ system had a linear relationship in the range of 0.5–50 μM for L-Cys (R = 0.9963) with the detection limit of 0.31 μM for L-Cys. The concentration of in healthy human fluids is in the range from 50 to 300 μM for L-Cys in plasma [22], so the current approach met the detection requirements for L-Cys in human fluids. Simultaneously, the comparison experiments using different fluorescence approaches for the determination of L-Cys were carried out and the results are shown in Table S2. The results showed that the proposed approach displayed comparable or better linear range and detection limit.

The interference from other amino acids in the determination of L-Cys was also investigated using the current approach under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 , there was no obvious change observed in the presence of other amino acids, GSH and Hcy besides L-Cys. These results indicated the excellent selectivity of the proposed method due to the strong interaction between Cu2+ and L-Cys.

To examine the practicality of the current approach, Eu-GQDs were applied to the detection of L-Cys in buffer-diluted calf and human serum samples. Standard recovery experiments were also carried out. The serum samples were treated as described above in Section 2.6 to make sure the L-Cys concentration in the samples were in the linear range and to obtain quantitative recovery of the spiked L-Cys. The experimental results revealed that the fluorescence intensity recovered with the addition of serum samples. As shown in Table 1 , the results obtained using Eu-GQDs showed good consistence with those reported in the literature [23,24], which indicated the reliability and accuracy of this approach for the determination of L-Cys in practical sample analysis. Because of the environmentally-friendly and biocompatibility of Eu-GQDs, the proposed method shows promise for practical application in the ecological and biological fields.

In summary, we synthesized Eu-GQDs by treating 3D Eu-graphene with concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3. Results of various characterizations revealed that the Eu atoms were successfully introduced into the graphene structures with an atomic ratio of 2.54%. The introduction of Eu atoms into GQDs changed the electron density and surface chemical activities of GQDs, facilitating the coordination reaction between Cu2+ and the oxygen groups on the surface of the Eu-GQDs. The obtained Eu-GQDs were demonstrated to be a novel fluorescent material for label-free determination of Cu2+ and L-Cys with high sensitivity and selectivity. The presence of Cu2+ turned off the fluorescence intensity of Eu-GQDs owing to the coordination reaction between Cu2+ and Eu-GQDs, while subsequent addition of biothiols turned on the fluorescence intensity of Eu-GQDs because of the strong affinity of Cu2+ to L-Cys via Cu–S bond. The proposed approach was simple in design and could be carried out with a convenient “mix-and-detect” protocol for homogeneous detection of Cu2+ and L-Cys in 10 min. In addition, the results obtained using the current assay when applied to real serum samples suggested its practicability for diagnosis. In view of their excellent luminescence, photo-stability and water-solubility, Eu-GQDs promise wide applications in the environmental and biological fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Nature Scientific Foundation of China (No. 21375112), Program of Science and Technology of Xiamen for University Innovation (3502Z20143025), Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (PCSIRT, No. IRT13036) and NFFTBS (No. J1310024), which are gratefully acknowledged. We thank Professor John Hodgkiss of The University of Hong Kong for assistance with the English in the paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.08.011.

Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives

Oxygen reduction electrocatalysis using N-doped graphene quantum-dots

Boron-doped graphene quantum dots for selective glucose sensing based on the “abnormal” aggregation-induced photoluminescence enhancement
Surrounding media sensitive photoluminescence of boron-doped graphene quantum dots for highly fluorescent dyed crystals, chemical sensing and bioimaging
Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+
Sulphur doping: a facile approach to tune the electronic structure and optical properties of graphene quantum dots
Synthesis and photoluminescence of fluorinated graphene quantum dots
Multicolour light emission from chlorine-doped graphene quantum dots

Optical bifunctionality of europium-complexed luminescent graphene nanosheets
Synthesis and photoluminescence of three-dimensional europium-complexed graphene macroassembly
Tunable wide blue photoluminescence with europium decorated graphene
Highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ with lysine enhancing bovine serum albumin modified-carbon dots fluorescent probe
Turn-on persistent luminescence probe based on graphitic carbon nitride for imaging detection of biothiols in biological fluids
Ag nanoparticles-decorated graphene quantum dots for label-free, rapid and sensitive detection of Ag+ and biothiols
Carbon nanodots as a fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
A graphene quantum dot-based method for the highly sensitive and selective fluorescence turn on detection of biothiols
Oligonucleotide-stabilized fluorescent silver nanoclusters for sensitive detection of biothiols in biological fluids
